metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan's claim that archived targets qualify as readable pin subjects against `find_inbound_relationships`'s explicit filtering of archived tickets (line 103 of `_inbound.py`), and specify whether `PlanRelationSnapshot` bypasses or replaces that filtering.
evidence: Plan text: 'archived and empty-description states qualify'
src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py line 103: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`

The plan states PlanRelationSnapshot is 'Build[t] from one compiled ticket-state preload per review; reuse it for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery' — implying it uses or supersedes find_inbound_relationships, but the filtering difference is unaddressed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'archived and empty-description states qualify' as readable targets for pins, but find_inbound_relationships (_inbound.py line 103) explicitly filters out archived tickets: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`. The PlanRelationSnapshot must either bypass this function or implement its own traversal — but the plan does not acknowledge or resolve this contradiction.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'archived and empty-description states qualify'
scenarios: A child or prerequisite ticket that has been archived would be skipped by the existing inbound-discovery function but the plan requires it to produce a pin — the implementation will either miss the archived target or must re-implement discovery from scratch.
A developer following this plan would write PlanRelationSnapshot based on the existing find_inbound_relationships contract and discover archived targets are silently excluded, contradicting the AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Implementation section: 'PlanRelationSnapshot collects children and prerequisites directly from the subject's own compiled state (parent_id/deps) and the preloaded ticket_states_by_id, not via find_inbound_relationships, so archived-target filtering in that function is bypassed by design.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py to file_impact (or explicitly document why resign_plan_review is exempt from the unconditional-pin AC and how its manifests remain consistent).
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py:126: sig = attest.sign_plan_review(verdict, material=current_material, repo_root=repo_root)
resign.py is absent from the ticket's file_impact list
AC: 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations'
Plan: 'Always emit pins independently of project enforcement configuration'
findings.finding: resign_plan_review (resign.py) calls attest.sign_plan_review and thereby build_manifest, producing a new plan-review manifest. Under the plan's unconditional-pin-emission requirement (AC: 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations'), a re-sign via resign_plan_review would emit a manifest WITHOUT pin lines unless resign.py is also updated to build and pass a PlanRelationSnapshot. resign.py is not listed in file_impact.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Always emit pins independently of project enforcement configuration') and AC line 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations'; file_impact omits src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py
scenarios: An operator calls `rebar sign-review <ticket>` (resign path) after a gate run; the resulting manifest lacks pin lines, violating the unconditional-pin AC even though the plan intends all new certifications to carry pins.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add resign.py to file_impact and describe how it obtains/passes a PlanRelationSnapshot to sign_plan_review, or add an explicit AC exemption explaining that resign produces a 'pinless refresh' manifest variant and specify how readers distinguish the two.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Normalize fingerprint terminology to a single term (e.g. 'material fingerprint') used consistently in the format spec, Implementation body, and AC6.
evidence: Format spec: "plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>"
Implementation body: "existing narrow fingerprints"
AC6: "PlanRelationSnapshot exposes complete relationship membership plus related fingerprints"
findings.finding: The concept of a fingerprint is named inconsistently across sections: 'material-fingerprint' in the format spec, 'narrow fingerprints' in the Implementation body, and 'related fingerprints' in AC6, all apparently referring to the same artifact.
findings.location: Implementation (format spec line; PlanRelationSnapshot section) vs AC6
scenarios: An implementer reads AC6 as requiring PlanRelationSnapshot to expose a 'related fingerprints' collection that is distinct from the 'narrow fingerprints' already computed, and adds a redundant field.
A reviewer cannot confirm AC6 is satisfied without inferring the three names are synonyms.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'narrow fingerprints' and 'related fingerprints' with 'material fingerprint(s)' everywhere to match the format-spec token name <material-fingerprint>.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the fingerprint carrier field to PlanRelationSnapshot's Implementation definition: name the field (e.g., `fingerprints_by_id: dict[str, str]`), specify its value type, and describe how it is populated (e.g., via `material_fingerprint` per related ticket state).
evidence: AC line 6 introduces 'related fingerprints' as part of PlanRelationSnapshot
Implementation body lists exactly four fields: subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids — none is a fingerprint
No fingerprint field, type, or computation rule for PlanRelationSnapshot is given anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: AC line 6 says 'PlanRelationSnapshot exposes complete relationship membership plus related fingerprints', but the Implementation's four named fields (subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids) include no fingerprint carrier. 'Related fingerprints' appears only in the AC and is never defined as a field, type, or computed value in the Implementation.
findings.location: AC: 'PlanRelationSnapshot exposes complete relationship membership plus related fingerprints' / Implementation: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, and prerequisite_ids'
scenarios: An implementer adds fingerprint storage as an ad-hoc dict, breaking the contract the prerequisite criterion expects.
The bounded sign-time retry logic that depends on fingerprints from the snapshot has no defined data source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the abort mechanism for unreadable/unresolvable targets: name the exception type raised (or the structured verdict field set), state whether the review emits a DET-blocking result or propagates an exception to the orchestrator, and confirm whether the existing `ManifestFormatError` or a new error class is the signal.
evidence: Plan uses the word 'abort' without naming an exception class or error path
Existing abort patterns in pass1.py use LLMUnavailableError for systemic failures and silent drop for per-chunk non-systemic failures — neither is referenced
The plan says abort happens 'before LLM prerequisite evaluation' but does not say what the calling orchestrator sees or how it surfaces the failure to the caller
findings.finding: The plan says 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort' but never specifies the abort surface: no exception type is named, no review-verdict outcome is defined (e.g. does the review emit a DET-blocking result, raise an internal exception, or produce a structured error envelope?), and no fallback behavior is described if the abort itself fails.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort'
scenarios: One implementer raises a new RebarError subclass; another returns a FAIL verdict with a det_blocking result; a third raises ManifestFormatError — all three are consistent with 'abort' but produce incompatible behavior.
The DET floor's 'abstain on unexpected error' pattern (det_invariants.py line 20) would silently swallow the abort if an implementer re-uses that harness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or an explicit rationale for deferral) that exercises the phase-story signing path end-to-end, including a scenario where the before/after revision check catches a stale collection and retries or aborts.
evidence: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.' — new locking/retry logic with observable failure modes.
Verification prescribes only unit tests and 'make check'; no integration or end-to-end scenario that triggers the before/after revision mismatch path or the lock re-verification path is described.
findings.finding: The new phase-story code path (outside-lock collection with before/after revision equality check, global-lock HEAD re-verification before signature append+commit) is a functional cutover of the signing flow, but no AC exercises this path end-to-end as it would run in production — only unit tests are prescribed.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision…' paragraph; Verification section
scenarios: The revision-equality check could be implemented incorrectly (e.g. always passes, or always fails) and the unit tests — which mock the lock and store — would remain green.
The global-lock HEAD re-verification step could be skipped on a code path that never acquires the lock; offline tests would not catch this.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace vague test category descriptions with concrete commands (e.g. `pytest tests/test_manifest_pins.py tests/test_plan_relation_snapshot.py -v`) anchored to each AC, including the pre-feature-reader fixture AC.
evidence: 'Run the focused manifest, attestation, and relationship-collection unit tests.' — no file paths, selectors, or expected output stated.
'Run make check and the non-integration test suite.' — generic catch-all, not anchored to any specific AC.
findings.finding: The Verification section names test categories ('focused manifest, attestation, and relationship-collection unit tests') but provides no concrete commands, test-module paths, or pytest/unittest selectors, leaving no reproducible way to prove individual ACs are satisfied.
findings.location: Verification section
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm which test proves AC 'Alias, short-ID, duplicate-edge, inbound-blocks, outgoing-depends_on, and archived-target cases are deterministic' or AC 'Pre-feature reader behavior ignores additive pin lines without invalidating the manifest'.
An agent executing the plan has no stopping condition for verification — 'make check passes' could be green even if the new pin-emission logic is never exercised.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a table or list mapping each AC to a specific proving command, e.g.:
- AC 'Passing reviews emit deterministic pins for every direct child': `pytest tests/test_manifest_pins.py::test_child_pins_deterministic -v`
- AC 'Pre-feature reader behavior ignores additive pin lines': `pytest tests/test_legacy_reader.py::test_pre_feature_pin_passthrough -v`
- etc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit Verification entries (or AC sub-items) naming unit tests for each ManifestFormatError sub-case and each abort condition (missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch), so failure-path coverage is part of the defined-of-done rather than assumed.
evidence: 'Run the focused manifest, attestation, and relationship-collection unit tests.' — no new failure-path tests named

'manifest_pins() returns [] only when absent and raises ManifestFormatError for any duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token, invalid canonical ID, or invalid fingerprint' — new function, new error paths
'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort' — new abort behavior with no named test work
findings.finding: The Verification section names only existing test suites ('focused manifest, attestation, and relationship-collection unit tests') without explicitly requiring new failure-path tests for the new manifest_pins() error cases (ManifestFormatError sub-cases: duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token, invalid canonical ID, invalid fingerprint) and the abort conditions (missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch targets). Since manifest_pins() and the abort flow are new logic with enumerated failure modes, the plan should explicitly call out test additions for these paths.
findings.location: Verification section; Implementation ¶1 (ManifestFormatError sub-cases); AC 'Unreadable or unresolvable targets stop review before LLM prerequisite evaluation'
scenarios: A ManifestFormatError variant (e.g. unknown role) is silently not tested; the implementation ships with a gap in that branch
The abort-before-LLM-evaluation path is untested; a regression re-orders evaluation and LLM is called on an unresolvable target, which goes unnoticed in CI
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Verification section with: 'Add unit tests for each ManifestFormatError variant (duplicate pin, unknown role, missing token, extra token, invalid canonical ID, invalid fingerprint) and for each abort condition (missing/deleted target, ambiguous reference, reducer error, canonical-ID mismatch), asserting that LLM prerequisite evaluation is not reached in the abort cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one observability instrument (structured log line or metric counter) for each new failure mode: revision-mismatch retry, target-abort (with reason), and ManifestFormatError during pin collection.
evidence: 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort' — abort is specified but no log/metric/alert is mentioned
'manifest_pins() … raises ManifestFormatError for any duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token…' — error surface is defined but observability is absent
Absence: no mention of structured logging, metrics emission, or alerting for any new failure point in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces new failure points (revision mismatch, unresolvable-target abort, ManifestFormatError during pin collection, ticket-store read errors) but names no observability mechanism — no log line, metric, or trace — so operators cannot distinguish a pin-collection abort from a normal review failure in production.
findings.location: Implementation section and Verification section
scenarios: A ticket-store read error silently aborts a review; on-call has no signal to distinguish it from a normal prerequisite skip.
Repeated revision mismatches are invisible; the retry mechanism exhausts without any trace in monitoring.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a structured log entry at WARN level for each retry attempt (including revision before/after), and at ERROR for abort conditions, carrying the canonical ID and failure reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the module that owns ManifestFormatError (e.g., manifest.py alongside the other format-parsing code) so callers and the re-export block in attest.py can be updated consistently.
evidence: ManifestFormatError not found in any search across src/rebar
file_impact names manifest.py but plan text never assigns ManifestFormatError a home module
attest.py imports from manifest.py via explicit re-export block — a cross-module placement would require updating that block
findings.finding: ManifestFormatError is named as a new exception raised by manifest_pins(), but no module home is specified in the plan. The symbol does not currently exist anywhere in the codebase, and the file_impact list names manifest.py as the natural owner — but the plan text never makes this explicit. If an implementer places it elsewhere (e.g., rebar._errors, a new exceptions.py), callers in attest.py and orchestrator.py will need a cross-module import not described by the plan.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'manifest_pins() returns [] only when absent and raises ManifestFormatError for any duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token, invalid canonical ID, or invalid fingerprint'
scenarios: Implementer places ManifestFormatError in rebar._errors; callers in attest.py and orchestrator.py need explicit imports not described by the plan, leading to a missing-import defect.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Implementation section: 'Define ManifestFormatError in manifest.py and re-export it from attest.py alongside the other manifest parsers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering manifest_pins() error behavior: specifically that malformed pin lines (duplicate role+id, unknown role, wrong token count, invalid canonical ID, invalid fingerprint) raise ManifestFormatError rather than being silently dropped or ignored.
evidence: "manifest_pins() returns [] only when absent and raises ManifestFormatError for any duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token, invalid canonical ID, or invalid fingerprint"
No AC mentions ManifestFormatError, malformed pin rejection, or error-on-parse behavior
findings.finding: The Implementation specifies a concrete error-handling contract for manifest_pins() — ManifestFormatError on duplicate, unknown role, missing/extra token, invalid canonical ID, or invalid fingerprint — but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies this parsing-error behavior.
findings.location: Implementation (manifest_pins contract) vs Acceptance Criteria (none covering ManifestFormatError)
scenarios: An implementer writes manifest_pins() silently dropping malformed lines rather than raising; no AC fails because error behavior is untested.
A reviewer trying to verify the implementation has no criterion to check against.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: "manifest_pins() raises ManifestFormatError for any pin line with a duplicate role+ID pair, unrecognized role, wrong number of tokens, invalid canonical ID format, or invalid fingerprint format; it returns [] for a manifest with no pin lines."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite the exact fingerprint specification (module, function name, or ADR section) in both the Implementation section and AC #3 so the format is unambiguous without codebase archaeology.
evidence: 'existing narrow fingerprints' — adjective 'existing' implies the spec lives elsewhere, but no pointer (module, ADR, doc section) is given.
Implementation also uses the phrase 'existing narrow fingerprints' without further elaboration.
findings.finding: AC #3 references 'existing narrow fingerprints' without citing where their format or algorithm is specified; an executor must discover this from the codebase rather than the plan, making the criterion's done-condition implicit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the fingerprint format for pin lines uses a different algorithm than the 'existing' one, producing non-interoperable manifests that still pass the vague AC.
A reviewer checking the AC cannot confirm correctness without hunting through the codebase for the fingerprint definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'existing narrow fingerprints' with a concrete reference such as 'fingerprints as produced by `compute_plan_material_fingerprint()` in `manifest.py` (see ADR-XXXX)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC4 to explicitly state whether inbound-block relationship targets receive a pin (and under which role) or are intentionally excluded from pin emission, so the criterion is unambiguously verifiable.
evidence: AC4: "Alias, short-ID, duplicate-edge, inbound-blocks, outgoing-depends_on, and archived-target cases are deterministic"
Implementation: "reuse it for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery" — no statement on whether inbound-block tickets are pinned
Pins are defined for roles 'child' and 'prerequisite' only; inbound-blocks is a third relationship direction not named as a pin role
findings.finding: AC4 names 'inbound-blocks' as a case that must be deterministic, but the Implementation only describes PlanRelationSnapshot as using inbound-blocks discovery internally — it does not state whether inbound-block relationships produce pins, are excluded from pins, or are merely handled without error. This creates ambiguity about what 'deterministic' means for that case.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 vs Implementation (PlanRelationSnapshot section)
scenarios: Implementer infers inbound-block tickets should be pinned under a 'prerequisite' role (they block this ticket); or infers they are explicitly excluded. Both are reasonable readings.
Test author writes a test asserting inbound-block tickets are NOT pinned; another writes asserting they ARE — both pass AC4 as written.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design doc) capturing the PlanRelationSnapshot contract, pin-line manifest format, and bounded sign-time retry pattern before or alongside implementation.
evidence: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract' — new cross-component contract with no doc artifact named
'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — new manifest format extension with no ADR or design-doc update specified
'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision' — new pipeline stage with no documentation plan
Absence: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel PlanRelationSnapshot contract, a new pin-line manifest format, and a bounded sign-time retry pattern — all new architectural decisions crossing component boundaries — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to record them.
findings.location: Implementation section (PlanRelationSnapshot contract, manifest pin format, phase story)
scenarios: A future contributor modifies the manifest parser without knowing the pin-line contract, breaking determinism silently.
The sign-time retry bound is misunderstood by a maintainer who tightens or removes it without knowing its purpose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief AGENTS.md or ADR section describing: (1) the pin-line manifest extension format and sort invariants, (2) PlanRelationSnapshot fields and lifecycle, (3) the sign-time revision-check retry rationale and its bounds.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the bounded sign-time retry precisely: maximum attempt count, retry trigger (revision mismatch), and behavior on exhaustion (surface error, abort review, or roll back manifest) so the write path is fully specified.
evidence: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — describes the check but not the retry loop
'The pin story does not acquire a separate sign lock' — no retry detail given
Absence: no max-retry count, no backoff interval, no 'abort review' vs 'surface error to caller' specification when retries are exhausted
findings.finding: 'Bounded sign-time retry' is asserted in the phase story but the plan never specifies the bound (max attempts), retry trigger condition, backoff strategy, or behavior when retries are exhausted — leaving an underspecified failure mode in a write-path state machine.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision… brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions'
scenarios: A high-write ticket store causes repeated revision mismatches; the retry loop spins indefinitely or terminates silently without surfacing an error.
Retry exhaustion causes a half-written manifest that is neither rolled back nor flagged, leaving the review in an ambiguous signed/unsigned state.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify, e.g., max 3 revision-mismatch retries with immediate re-collection; on exhaustion, abort review with a ManifestRetryExhaustedError and leave the manifest unmodified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the sign-time retry bound (e.g., max N attempts with backoff), and define the terminal error type and message surfaced to the caller when the bound is exceeded.
evidence: Plan text: 'PlanRelationSnapshot carries the ticket-store generation used for bounded sign-time retry'

The plan specifies the guard mechanism (before/after HEAD equality + lock-time verification) but nowhere defines what 'bounded' concretely means: no count, no timeout, no backoff, no terminal error type.
T9 checklist: 'operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once' and 'Update concurrency / ownership is clear' — the retry termination path is part of concurrency ownership.
findings.finding: The plan names 'bounded sign-time retry' (using ticket_store_revision as a generation guard) but does not specify the retry bound — neither a maximum attempt count, a time limit, nor a backoff policy — and does not define what error or outcome is surfaced to the caller when that bound is exceeded. Under concurrent review execution with persistent HEAD churn, the retry loop's termination condition is undefined, leaving the system in an unspecified state (silent failure, hang, or exception without actionable context) when the bound triggers.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision' — 'PlanRelationSnapshot carries the ticket-store generation used for bounded sign-time retry'
scenarios: Concurrent review execution: two or more reviews are signed in rapid succession, causing the ticket-store HEAD to advance repeatedly; the retrying review never observes a stable HEAD within any unspecified bound and either loops indefinitely or surfaces an opaque error with no guidance on resolution.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete retry policy to the PlanRelationSnapshot spec: e.g., 'retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff; on exhaustion raise SignTimeConflictError with the ticket_store_revision values observed, advising the caller to re-initiate the review'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Motivation / Current Behavior' section that concretely describes what goes wrong today without plan-material pins (e.g., a scenario where a signed review is bound to incorrect or stale material, or a correctness/integrity gap), so the necessity of each mechanism — especially the abort-before-LLM guard and sign-time retry — is grounded.
evidence: Goal states only 'Bind every newly certified plan review to the narrow plan material of its direct children and direct prerequisites' — no problem statement explaining what goes wrong without this binding.
No section describes current behavior, a failing scenario, or a concrete gap in the existing review system.
ABSENCE: no reproduction steps, no Expected/Actual, no named defect or incident motivating the change.
findings.finding: The plan adds a non-trivial mechanism (pin emission, PlanRelationSnapshot, sign-time retry, manifest format extension, abort-before-LLM guard) but never motivates why the current behavior is wrong or insufficient — no reproduction, no Expected/Actual, no named defect, no stated problem that the absence of pins causes today.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation sections
scenarios: Without a stated motivation, reviewers and implementers cannot assess whether the mechanism is proportionate or whether a simpler approach (e.g., existing signature coverage) would suffice.
The abort-before-LLM guard in particular alters existing review workflow; its necessity is undemonstrated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Problem / Current Behavior' section, e.g.: 'Today, a certified plan-review DSSE envelope contains no cryptographic binding to the specific ticket states of its children or prerequisites at review time. A verifier cannot distinguish a review signed against the current plan material from one signed against a materially different prior state. This allows silent drift between what was reviewed and what is later executed. The pins close this gap.' Then link each implementation element back to this problem.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm PlanRelationSnapshot has ≥2 distinct non-trivial consumers beyond the sign path, or justify why extending PlanContext (or passing a plain `ticket_states_by_id` dict) is insufficient.
evidence: Search for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' across codebase returns zero hits — no existing call-sites.
PlanContext (det_floor.py:104) already carries children + state dict; ticket deps are accessible via state.get('deps').

The plan names exactly one consumer: 'Build it from one compiled ticket-state preload per review; reuse it for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery. The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object.'
Rule-of-Three: one consumer named, none existing.
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is a new named dataclass with a single declared consumer (the plan-review sign path) and zero existing call-sites, violating the Rule-of-Three for non-trivial abstractions. PlanContext already carries `children` (from `list_tickets`) and `state` (which contains `deps`/`parent_id`). The plan could instead extend `PlanContext` or inline the needed fields (child fingerprints + dep-relation iteration) directly into the signing step without a separate contract type.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, and prerequisite_ids.'
scenarios: A future maintainer must learn and maintain a new contract type that duplicates fields already present in PlanContext, for a single use case.
If the prerequisite criterion is the only other named consumer, it could receive the same preloaded dict directly without a typed wrapper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the existing write lock in `_store/lock.py` is insufficient (i.e., document the specific concurrent-write scenario the before/after revision check guards against), or simplify to rely on the existing lock.
evidence: Search for 'ticket_store_revision', 'store_revision', 'ensure_fresh', 'clean tickets tracker' across codebase: zero hits.
The store lock mechanism (src/rebar/_store/lock.py) serialises writes but has no HEAD-equality polling pattern.
The sign_manifest path in signing.py / attest.py / sign_plan_review() does not currently perform any revision-equality check.
No existing 'outside-lock collection / inside-lock verify' pattern anywhere in the codebase.
findings.finding: The 'phase story' outside-lock/inside-lock ticket_store_revision equality protocol is a new optimistic-concurrency mechanism with zero analogues in the codebase. The plan introduces 'the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh', 'equal before/after revisions', and 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — none of these constructs (`ticket_store_revision`, `ensure_fresh`, HEAD-equality polling) exist in the codebase. This is a non-trivial novel concurrency pattern built from scratch.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh. The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
scenarios: The protocol could be implemented incorrectly or be redundant if the existing write lock already provides sufficient serialization guarantees.
The 'before/after revision equality' check may never fire in practice if the store is rarely written concurrently, making it premature complexity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'existing narrow fingerprints' concretely in the plan — either name the specific function (e.g., `current_material_fingerprint`) and its call site, or introduce the new fingerprint function explicitly; remove the word 'existing' if this is a new artifact.
evidence: AC line: 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints.'
Grep for 'narrow fingerprint' / 'narrow_fingerprint' / 'narrow.*fingerprint' returns zero matches across the entire codebase.
Grep for 'material_fingerprint' shows it is the plan-material hash (pass1.py line 551–562), but it is never described as 'narrow'.
Implementation section uses the term '<material-fingerprint>' in the pin line format but never equates it to an existing function.
findings.finding: The AC states 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints', but no 'narrow fingerprint' function or concept exists anywhere in the codebase. The closest existing artifact is `current_material_fingerprint` (a per-ticket plan-material hash), but this requires a full ticket context read and is never called 'narrow'. The plan never defines what 'existing narrow fingerprints' means or where it comes from.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints.'
scenarios: An implementer reading this plan cannot determine what function to call to obtain the 'narrow fingerprint' for a pin line — they may invent a new function, reuse current_material_fingerprint, or use a different hash, all without spec.
The AC criterion 'existing narrow fingerprints' is untestable as written because the referent is unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `ticket_store_revision` (the tracker HEAD SHA read after `ensure_fresh`) is new infrastructure to be introduced, not an existing capability; add it to the explicit implementation deliverables.
evidence: Plan text: 'ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh'
Grep for 'ticket_store_revision', 'tracker.*HEAD SHA', 'store_revision', 'store_generation', 'tracker_head' returns zero matches.
ensure_fresh in _engine_support/reads.py (line 179) performs a throttled fetch+reconverge but does NOT return or expose a HEAD SHA — it returns None.
No existing code in the sign_plan_review / sign_manifest path reads the tracker HEAD SHA.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh' as if this is a retrievable existing value, but no such 'tracker HEAD SHA' read pattern exists anywhere after ensure_fresh in the current signing or plan-review paths. The plan treats this as an already-available concept when it is entirely new infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh.'
scenarios: An implementer expecting ticket_store_revision to already exist will search the codebase and find nothing — the plan's 'concretely' framing implies this is a current capability, not new work.
The bounded sign-time retry the plan builds on this concept has no existing foundation to reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the function or git-ref expression that reads the ticket-store tracker HEAD SHA for ticket_store_revision (e.g., call site in reads.py or a new helper), so the PlanRelationSnapshot implementation has a concrete anchor.
evidence: src/rebar/signing.py:524: def head_sha(repo_root) — reads CODE repo HEAD, not ticket tracker HEAD
No existing function named tracker_head, ticket_store_revision, or equivalent found in src/rebar
ensure_fresh in src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:179 exists but returns None, not a SHA
findings.finding: ticket_store_revision is described as 'the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh', but no existing function reads the ticket-store tracker branch HEAD SHA. The closest symbol is rebar.signing.head_sha(), which reads the CODE repository HEAD, not the ticket-store orphan-branch HEAD. The plan does not name the module, function, or git ref (e.g., refs/heads/tickets) through which this value is obtained, leaving the implementation anchor unspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh'
scenarios: Implementer must invent a new function to read the tracker-branch HEAD SHA; without a named anchor they may use the wrong git ref or the wrong repo, causing the revision guard to compare incomparable values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the recovery action when the under-lock HEAD check detects a revision change: whether the review aborts (and with what error), silently re-collects and re-signs, or escalates to a full re-run, so implementers have a single unambiguous path.
evidence: Plan text: 'brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit'
No recovery action is described when before-revision != after-revision
LLM prerequisite evaluation sits between the outside-lock snapshot and the under-lock check — this window can be tens of seconds, making revision drift a realistic scenario
The existing sign path (sign_manifest → stage_and_commit in event_append.py) takes the write lock only at commit time, confirming the window exists
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'before/after HEAD equals' TOCTOU guard but does not specify what happens on a mismatch: whether the review aborts with an error, silently re-collects pins and re-signs, or triggers a full re-run. Without this, the retry/failure path is unimplementable from the plan text alone.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
scenarios: A concurrent reviewer edits a prerequisite ticket between pin-collection and the under-lock HEAD check; the plan gives no guidance on whether to abort, retry, or accept the now-stale pins
A background push/sync changes HEAD between the snapshot read and the lock acquisition; implementers will make incompatible choices
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'If the under-lock HEAD check detects a changed revision, abort pin emission and fail the signing step with a retryable StoreError (the review caller re-runs from scratch); do not silently accept stale pins or partially re-collect.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the Implementation section which relation types constitute the 'prerequisite' role (e.g., 'ticket's own `depends_on` deps plus any ticket holding a `blocks` edge targeting the subject'), so the relation-to-role mapping is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan text defines only two roles ('child' and 'prerequisite') without mapping relation types to roles
AC4 test cases name 'inbound-blocks' and 'outgoing-depends_on' as required cases, implying these are the prerequisite sources
Codebase has 7 canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, caused_by (types.py:24)
ABSENCE: no sentence in the Implementation section states 'prerequisite_ids are sourced from depends_on + inbound blocks relations'
findings.finding: The plan never enumerates which ticket relation types map to the 'prerequisite' role. AC4 implies `blocks`-inbound and `depends_on`-outbound qualify, but the Implementation section is silent on whether `supersedes`, `relates_to`, `caused_by`, or `discovered_from` edges also create prerequisite pins.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Roles are exactly child and prerequisite' and AC4 — 'inbound-blocks, outgoing-depends_on, and archived-target cases are deterministic'
scenarios: An implementer includes `relates_to` edges in prerequisite_ids, producing pins for non-blocking dependencies and widening the pinned set beyond the intended scope
An implementer excludes inbound `blocks` (only reading the subject's own `depends_on` deps) and misses half the required prerequisite set
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'prerequisite_ids are the union of (a) the subject ticket\'s own deps with relation==\'depends_on\' and (b) any other ticket whose deps include relation==\'blocks\' with target_id==subject — exactly the two sources in graph._blockers._find_direct_blockers. No other relation types contribute to the prerequisite role.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Implementation section explaining why pin lines are embedded as manifest steps (rather than a separate event or record field): specifically that this keeps pins inside the DSSE op-cert signature boundary with zero schema change, following the existing dep/regver/verified-at-sha step pattern.
evidence: ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan explains why manifest steps were chosen over a new signed DSSE payload field
ABSENCE: no rationale for why PlanRelationSnapshot is a new typed contract rather than extending the existing PlanContext
ABSENCE: no explanation for why git tickets-branch HEAD is the right revision anchor for the bounded retry window
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why pin lines are placed as manifest steps (vs. a new signed payload field, a separate event, or a record-level annotation). The absence of a stated reason leaves implementers unable to judge future changes against design intent — for example, whether a future migration to a standalone pin event would be safe.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines: plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
scenarios: A future implementer proposes moving pin data to a separate event for cleaner separation; without the rationale, they cannot evaluate whether the DSSE signature coverage requirement would be broken
An implementer adds pin lines AFTER dep lines (reversing the specified order) because the plan doesn't explain that the order enforces the 'before dep lines' determinism contract

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or inline design note justifying the outside-lock collection + HEAD-SHA bounded-retry scheme over simply holding the unified write lock across pin collection, and confirm what TOCTOU window (if any) remains after the under-lock HEAD re-verification.
evidence: Plan text: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit. The pin story does not acquire a separate sign lock.'

Existing pattern: rebar._store.lock (unified fcntl+mkdir dual-leg lock on .ticket-write.lock) is the single write-path lock; all mutations go through it (lock → reduce+verify → write → commit in rebar._commands.txn).
No ADR or prior-art citation accompanies this deviation from the unified-lock pattern.
The plan does not explain what failure mode the before/after HEAD-SHA check closes that the unified lock alone would not.
findings.finding: The 'phase story' describes a bespoke optimistic-locking concurrency scheme — collect HEAD SHA before pin collection, re-verify the SAME HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit — without citing prior art or justifying it over the simpler established pattern of holding the existing unified write lock (rebar._store.lock) across the whole collection+sign operation. This is a novel concurrent-read-then-locked-verify pattern not used elsewhere in the codebase and not backed by an ADR.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit. The pin story does not acquire a separate sign lock.'
scenarios: If the HEAD-SHA equality check is not atomic with the lock acquisition, a concurrent write between the second HEAD read and the lock claim could produce a false-equal check and allow a stale pin to be signed.
If the rationale for avoiding the sign lock is performance (long-running reduction), that tradeoff should be documented so future maintainers do not simplify it away incorrectly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific reduction mechanism (e.g. reduce_ticket per related ID, or a new targeted reduce_tickets(ids) helper) that backs the 'one compiled ticket-state preload per review' assertion, and confirm it supports alias + canonical-ID resolution for the target set.
evidence: Plan text: 'Build it from one compiled ticket-state preload per review; reuse it for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery.'
Codebase has reduce_ticket (single ticket) and reduce_all_tickets (full store batch), but no named targeted-batch preload for plan-review that reduces only the subject + direct relations.
ABSENCE: no existing 'ticket_states_by_id' or equivalent partial-preload function found in src/rebar.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Build it from one compiled ticket-state preload per review' for PlanRelationSnapshot without identifying what existing mechanism produces this preload. The codebase has reduce_ticket (per-ticket) and reduce_all_tickets (full store), but no named batch-preload for the plan-review path. The plan does not verify whether reduce_all_tickets is the intended mechanism (which scans the entire store, not just the subject + its direct relations) or whether a narrower reduction is needed.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, and prerequisite_ids. Build it from one compiled ticket-state preload per review'
scenarios: If reduce_all_tickets is used, every plan review pays the cost of reducing the entire store — potentially expensive at scale.
If a new targeted reduction is needed, its correctness for alias/canonical-ID resolution (which the plan relies on) is an unverified capability assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the prerequisite criterion: assign it a criterion ID (to be added to CANONICAL_LLM or as a project criterion), add a routing entry in criteria_routing.json, specify its structured output schema (Pydantic model or JSON schema) and register_contract call, co-located with the PlanRelationSnapshot field that feeds it.
evidence: Zero hits for 'prerequisite' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json
Zero hits for 'prerequisite' in src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py or registry.py
CANONICAL_LLM frozenset in registry.py (lines 95–173) contains no prerequisite criterion id
Plan says 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object' and 'Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation' — criterion is referenced but never named, routed, or schema'd
findings.finding: The prerequisite LLM evaluation referenced in the Implementation ('Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation') and consumed by 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object' has no corresponding criterion definition anywhere: no criterion ID in CANONICAL_LLM, no entry in criteria_routing.json, no output schema or register_contract call exists in the codebase or the plan text.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object' / AC line: 'Unreadable or unresolvable targets stop review before LLM prerequisite evaluation'
scenarios: An implementer must invent a criterion ID, routing entry, output schema, and register_contract call that the plan never specifies, making the prerequisite evaluation step unimplementable from the plan alone.
The plan's ordering guarantee ('run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation') is unenforceable without a concrete criterion the orchestrator can route to.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific fingerprint function (e.g., `material_fingerprint(ctx)` from pass1.py) that produces the `<material-fingerprint>` token in each `plan-material-pin:` line, including its expected length/format, co-located with the manifest line format definition.
evidence: Plan defines the manifest line format as 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' without specifying which function produces `<material-fingerprint>`
pass1.py line 551: `def material_fingerprint(ctx: PlanContext) -> str:` returns a 16-hex SHA-256 truncation keyed on description/file_impact/children
manifest.py lines 171–224: `build_manifest` writes dep hashes as 'dep <sha256> <path>' — a different per-file fingerprint scheme
Neither function is referenced by name in the plan's format spec
findings.finding: The AC states 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints' and the Implementation says 'existing narrow fingerprints', but neither the plan nor the Implementation names which fingerprint function/format populates the `<material-fingerprint>` token in a `plan-material-pin:` line. The codebase has at least two candidate 'narrow' fingerprints: the 16-hex `material_fingerprint` from pass1.py and per-file SHA-256 dep hashes from build_manifest.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' / AC: 'Pins use canonical IDs, exact roles, and existing narrow fingerprints'
scenarios: An implementer may use the per-ticket material_fingerprint (16 hex) or a per-child dep hash or a new hash, producing interoperability failures when manifest_pins() is called by a different code path.
A verifier reading a legacy vs. new manifest cannot determine whether the token is a 16-char or 64-char hash.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation format spec, replace 'existing narrow fingerprints' with the explicit call: each pin's `<material-fingerprint>` is produced by calling `material_fingerprint(PlanContext)` for the target ticket, yielding the same 16-hex SHA-256 truncation already used by the subject ticket's own attestation.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
